title: G7 Demands AI Access Guarantees, OpenAI Joins Anthropic in the Gate, and China Courts the Developing World
subhead: G7 leaders push for trusted-partner AI access as the Mythos shutdown reverberates; OpenAI's GPT-5.6 gets a limited release under White House pressure; China counters with a global cooperation pitch; and Singapore's AI-driven exports surge 38%.

sections.heading: G7 Wants AI Access Without a Kill Switch
sections.body:
At the G7 summit in Evian-les-Bains, French President Emmanuel Macron and Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi both pressed the US over its ability to revoke AI model access overnight, according to TechCrunch and Reuters. Macron warned over lunch with Trump, Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei, and OpenAI CEO Sam Altman that if the US 'from one day to the next can turn off the switch,' it would harm both European customers and the AI firms themselves. Modi echoed the concern, telling leaders that democratic nations must have unfettered access to top AI models to protect critical infrastructure, the Financial Times reported.

G7 leaders discussed creating a trusted-partners scheme that would grant non-US nations access to advanced American AI models from firms like Anthropic and OpenAI, provided they use them to strengthen defenses against rivals like China, Reuters reported. The framework remains vague on which countries or companies would qualify, and whether it addresses the core concern — that access remains a unilateral US decision.

Cohere CEO Aidan Gomez framed the issue bluntly: 'Companies and democratic nations remaining dependent on a small handful of big tech companies is dangerous to resilience... It is about who controls the foundational technology that will shape our economic security and national sovereignty for decades to come,' he told TechCrunch.
sections.whyItMatters: The Mythos/Fable export ban has shifted from a US regulatory story into a alliance cohesion problem. Allies are not just asking for access — they are demanding structural guarantees that access cannot be revoked unilaterally, which challenges the entire premise of US export control authority.

sections.heading: OpenAI's GPT-5.6 Gets a Gate of Its Own
sections.body:
The Trump administration has asked OpenAI to limit the release of its upcoming GPT-5.6 Sol model to a small group of 20 government-trusted partners before any wider rollout, Bloomberg and Axios reported. The request came from the White House's Office of the National Cyber Director and the Office of Science and Technology Policy, which want time to develop a framework for evaluating the security of frontier models.

The move follows the June 12 shutdown of Anthropic's Mythos 5 and Fable 5 models to global users on national security grounds. According to the Daily Caller News Foundation, the government limited GPT-5.6's rollout because it 'may have capabilities like Anthropic's Mythos 5.' Altman reportedly stressed that OpenAI staff must work with the administration on safety restrictions, 'even if the company disagrees,' a person familiar told Bloomberg.

Anthropic, for its part, pushed back on the Mythos shutdown, saying: 'We disagree that the finding of a narrow potential jailbreak should be the cause for recalling a commercial model deployed to hundreds of millions of people. If this standard was employed across the industry, we believe it would essentially halt all new model deployments for all frontier model providers,' per the company's public statement.
sections.whyItMatters: What started as a single enforcement action against Anthropic has become a systemic practice. The White House is establishing a de facto pre-release review process for frontier models — voluntary in name, but functionally unavoidable for companies that want government contracts and trust.

sections.heading: China Pitches Free AI to the Developing World Against G7 Gatekeeping
sections.body:
As G7 leaders debated who gets access to American AI, China moved in the opposite direction. Top diplomat Wang Yi released a global governance whitepaper proposing the creation of a world AI cooperation organization and offering cheap or free AI models to developing nations, according to China Daily and The Next Web. The proposal explicitly criticizes trade restrictions that 'politicize technology' and positions Beijing as an advocate for open, inclusive AI governance.

The pitch targets precisely the countries the G7's trusted-partners framework risks leaving out — nations that lack leverage to negotiate preferential US access but need AI capabilities for economic development. Wang framed it as a choice between cooperation and fragmentation.

The counteroffer comes as the US holds off on blacklisting DeepSeek and more than 100 other flagged firms. Reuters reported that an interagency committee approved DeepSeek, memory chipmaker CXMT, and others for addition to the Commerce Department's Entity List last year, but the Trump administration has not published those additions. Analysts see the delay as a calculated move to avoid escalating tensions with Beijing, even as officials accuse DeepSeek of supporting China's military and attempting to access restricted US technology through shell companies.
sections.whyItMatters: Two competing global AI architectures are crystallizing: a US-led system where access is conditional and revocable, and a Chinese system where access is cheap and unrestricted but comes with data and governance entanglements. The developing world is the battleground.

sections.heading: Singapore's AI Export Surge Hits 38%, Driven by Data Center Buildout
sections.body:
Singapore's non-oil domestic exports rose 38.4% year-on-year in May, the sharpest growth in over two decades, according to Enterprise Singapore. Electronics exports led the expansion, rising 94.8%, with integrated circuits up 80.9%, disk media products up 227.8%, and personal computers up 140.9%.

EToro market analyst Zavier Wong traced the surge to a 'single concentrated force': Microsoft, Alphabet, Meta, and Amazon collectively lifting their 2026 capital expenditure plans to more than US$700 billion. Singapore's semiconductor industry association executive director Ang Wee Seng noted that a significant portion of the electronics uplift is 'either directly or indirectly connected to AI demand,' spanning logic chips, memory, storage, and networking hardware.

Exports to Taiwan surged 135.2%, to the US 80.9%, and to China 31%. Only Indonesia contracted, falling 26.9% on weak domestic demand. OCBC chief economist Selena Ling cautioned that the pace may be difficult to sustain as base effects rise in the second half of the year.
sections.whyItMatters: Singapore is the clearest real-time indicator that the AI infrastructure buildout has become the dominant force in global electronics trade. The $700B hyperscaler capex commitment is reshaping supply chains — and making small, trusted trade nodes like Singapore disproportionately important.

sections.heading: G7 Minerals Push: Japan Proposes Joint Stockpiling as China Tightens Grip
sections.body:
Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi proposed a Joint Stockpiling Collaboration Initiative for critical minerals at the G7 summit, according to MSN and Yahoo News. The proposal came as G7 leaders launched an action plan to secure critical mineral supply chains after China moved to further tighten its control over rare earth exports. Canada and Japan also signed agreements to reduce dependency on Chinese rare-earth minerals, with Canadian and Japanese companies inking deals during a trade mission to Tokyo.

A CSIS analysis noted that the G7's critical minerals ambitions face a practical challenge: Iran's natural resources have become a factor in the broader strategic competition, with Russia and China watching US-Iran negotiations closely for leverage implications in the minerals domain.
sections.whyItMatters: Japan is positioning itself as the architect of a G7 minerals resilience framework, but joint stockpiling without new mining and processing capacity is a bandage on a structural dependency. China's grip on rare earths remains the leverage point the G7 has yet to crack.

sections.heading: UK Bans Social Media for Under-16s, Germany Launches Sixth-Gen Fighter After FCAS Collapse
sections.body:
UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced a sweeping ban on social media for children under 16, covering Facebook, Instagram, X, TikTok, Snapchat, and YouTube. The ban, expected to take effect in spring 2027, also raises the minimum age for romantic chatbot interactions to 18 and bans livestreaming and stranger contact for under-16s across all platforms. WhatsApp and Signal are exempt. More than 90% of parents in the public consultation supported an outright ban, according to the UK government.

In defense aerospace, eight German companies including Airbus, Diehl Defence, Hensoldt, MTU Aero Engines, and MBDA Deutschland announced 'Team Gen 6' at ILA Berlin 2026 — a new industry grouping to develop a sixth-generation fighter aircraft following the collapse of the Franco-German FCAS/SCAF program, The Aviationist reported. Airbus released concept images of a twin-engine design with canard foreplanes and a diverterless supersonic inlet, operating alongside the newly unveiled U760 Ravenstorm unmanned combat drone. A parallel Spanish industry group is also forming with Indra, ITP Aero, and others.
sections.whyItMatters: The UK ban — following Australia's similar move last November — signals that governments are moving from content moderation to platform prohibition for minors. In European defense, FCAS's collapse means Germany is going its own way on next-gen airpower, splitting the European fighter ecosystem further at a time when the US F-35 program faces its own readiness crisis.

bottomLine: The G7's Evian summit crystallized a central tension in the AI sovereignty debate: allies want structural guarantees that the US cannot revoke model access overnight, but Washington is doubling down on exactly that control — now extending it from Anthropic to OpenAI's GPT-5.6. As the trusted-partners framework takes shape on one side and China pitches free AI to the developing world on the other, two incompatible global AI architectures are hardening. Singapore's 38% export surge shows where the money is flowing in the meantime: straight into AI infrastructure, with $700B in hyperscaler capex reshaping trade patterns in real time. The week's other signal: Europe is fragmenting on defense industrial policy (Team Gen 6 after FCAS collapse) and hardening on digital policy (UK's under-16 social media ban), while Japan tries to build a minerals resilience framework that still depends on Chinese supply.
sources: TechCrunch, Reuters, Bloomberg, Axios, Daily Caller News Foundation, Anthropic, Channel News Asia, China Daily, The Next Web, CSIS, Yahoo News, MSN, Wired UK, The Aviationist, Flight Global, CNBC, Independent (Macron/G7), Forbes, OCBC, Singapore Enterprise Singapore
